skill.name: verification-before-completion
skill.description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evide...
skill.summary: Enforces running verification commands before claiming work is complete. Requires fresh evidence from actual command output before stating success.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a behavioral skill that provides guidelines for AI verification practices. The static scanner flagged external command examples and cryptographic references, but these are false positives - the skill contains only documentation and example text in markdown format, not actual executable code. No real code execution, network requests, or file operations exist in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands Documentation (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Skill contains markdown examples showing verification commands like '[Run test command]'. These are illustrative documentation, not actual code execution. The backtick syntax is markdown formatting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill is a pure documentation/guidance file with no executable Ruby or shell code. The backticks are markdown code fences used for formatting examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner misidentified text content as cryptographic code. Lines 3 and 144 contain plain English descriptions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mentioned lines contain YAML frontmatter and plain text description. No cryptographic algorithms are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: System/Network Reconnaissance (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner misidentified references to 'agent reports' and 'human partner' as reconnaissance. These are actually warnings not to trust unverified agent success reports.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill warns against trusting agent reports without verification. This is the opposite of reconnaissance - it's about independent verification.

content.value_statement: This skill prevents false completion claims by requiring AI assistants to run verification commands and confirm output before stating work is done. It enforces evidence-based reporting.
actual_capabilities: Enforces running verification commands before claiming success
Requires fresh command output as evidence before stating completion
Prevents false claims based on assumptions or agent reports
Provides structured verification workflow (identify, run, read, verify, claim)
Lists common failure patterns and rationalizations to avoid
Applies to tests, builds, bug fixes, regression tests, and agent delegation
limitations: Does not execute verification commands itself - provides guidance only
Requires the AI to have access to run verification commands in the environment
Does not define which specific verification commands to use - depends on the project
Cannot verify work if no verification mechanism exists in the project

use_cases.title: Test Completion Verification
use_cases.description: Before claiming all tests pass, the AI must run the test command and show actual output with 0 failures.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using Claude or Codex for coding tasks

use_cases.title: Build Success Verification
use_cases.description: Before claiming a build succeeds, verify the exit code is 0 and review any build output.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers and developers running CI/CD

use_cases.title: Bug Fix Verification
use_cases.description: Before claiming a bug is fixed, run the original failing test or command to confirm it now passes.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers fixing defects

prompt_templates.title: Basic Verification Request
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to verify [component] is working. What command should I run to confirm it? After running, show me the exact output before claiming success.
prompt_templates.scenario: When unsure what verification command to use

prompt_templates.title: Test Completion Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Before we commit or create a PR, run the full test suite and show me the output. I need to see zero failures before claiming tests pass.

prompt_templates.title: Build Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the build command and check the exit code. Show me the output. Do not claim the build succeeds until you verify exit code is 0.

prompt_templates.title: Agent Delegation Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: An agent reported completing [task]. Do not trust this report. Check the VCS diff, verify the changes exist, and run verification commands to confirm actual completion.

output_examples.output: After running `npm test`, I see 34/34 tests passing with 0 failures. Exit code is 0. Tests pass.
After running `npm run build`, the exit code is 0 with no errors in output. Build succeeds.
After running the original failing command, the error no longer appears and the command exits with code 0. Bug is fixed.

best_practices: Always run verification commands fresh - never rely on previous runs or cached results
Show actual command output as evidence before making any success claim
Check exit codes, not just output text - a command can produce output but still fail
anti_patterns: Claiming success based on 'should pass' or 'probably works' without running verification
Trusting agent success reports without independent verification
Using partial verification (like linter passing) as proof of complete success

faq.question: What counts as verification evidence?
faq.answer: Fresh command output showing exit code 0, test results with 0 failures, or actual runtime behavior that confirms the claim.

faq.question: Can I skip verification if I am confident?
faq.answer: No. Confidence is not evidence. The skill explicitly states 'Confidence does not equal evidence.'

faq.question: What if no verification command exists?
faq.answer: Create one or state that verification is not possible. Do not claim completion without evidence.

faq.question: Does this apply to agent Delegation?
faq.answer: Yes. Always verify agent results independently. Check VCS diff and run verification commands yourself.

faq.question: What about partial verification like linter passing?
faq.answer: Partial verification is not sufficient. A passing linter does not mean the code compiles or tests pass.

faq.question: Can I claim completion after one test pass?
faq.answer: For regression tests, you must verify the red-green cycle: test fails, fix applied, test passes, then revert fix and confirm test fails again.
